summary: Fowler examines the alienating atmosphere in the tech community that contributes to diversity imbalance, particularly affecting women and historically discriminated-against groups. He acknowledges that as a middle-class white man, he rarely experiences this alienation, while accomplished women colleagues still feel unwelcome at tech gatherings. The post explores how sexual jokes, online harassment, and the accumulation of small slights create a hostile environment. Fowler argues that individuals can choose whether to care about offending others, and that the tech community should actively work to combat this atmosphere through awareness, codes of conduct, and speaking up against inappropriate behavior.
key_points: The alienating atmosphere is experienced very differently depending on one's identity - what feels neutral to majority groups can signal 'you're not welcome here' to others
Small individual incidents accumulate into significant harm through their sheer quantity (microaggressions)
While you cannot control whether someone is offended, you can choose whether you care about their reaction
The standard you walk past is the standard you accept - silence enables the problem to persist
Fear of online backlash creates its own form of censorship, silencing articulate people who don't want to deal with harassment
money_quotes: I can't choose whether someone is offended by my actions. I can choose whether I care.
The standard you walk past is the standard you accept.
Anyone driven away from expressing innovation or writing excellent code is a loss to all of us, however unoffended we think they should be.
Their reaction is a result of their life experiences, which will be different to mine.
While I cannot be responsible for what my ancestors did, I can take responsibility to play what small part I can in cleaning up their mess.
Ironically the bilers often complain about censorship infringing their right to make sexual jokes, but not acknowledging that fear of the intermobs imposes its own censorship.
Correcting these long-term discriminations takes time, and can't be solved by just saying 'we're all equal now'.
key_insight: Creating an inclusive tech community requires those in positions of privilege to actively care about and address behaviors that alienate others, even when those behaviors seem harmless from the majority perspective.
